%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Analog and Digital Signals

> **Section Objective:** Learn how analog/digital classification differs from continuous-time/discrete-time classification.

## Concepts In This Section

- Continuous-time versus discrete-time
- Analog versus digital
- M-ary and binary signals
- Quantization

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="eyJjcmFtIjoiVXNlIHRoaXMgdmlzdWFsIGZvciBmYXN0IHJlY29nbml0aW9uIG9mIHRoZSBrZXkgcGF0dGVybi4iLCJzdGFuZGFyZCI6IlVzZSB0aGlzIHZpc3VhbCB0byBjbGFyaWZ5IHRoZSBtYWluIGNvbmNlcHQuIiwidG9wX3Njb3JlIjoiVXNlIHRoaXMgdmlzdWFsIHRvIHN1cmZhY2UgYSBzdWJ0bGUgZGlzdGluY3Rpb24sIHRyYXAsIG9yIHZhcmlhbnQuIn0=" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Fig. 1.10](/figures/page-079-fig__1_10-1.png)
*Fig. 1.10 contrasts a smooth continuous-time signal with a discrete-time data sequence measured at quarterly intervals.*
<div class="lesson-figure-description">The figure has two panels. In panel (a), the signal is drawn as a smooth continuous curve for every time value along the horizontal axis, showing a continuous-time signal x(t). In panel (b), values appear only at separate quarterly time instants from 1981 to 1994, shown as individual bars representing GNP percent change. This figure is about the time axis — whether the signal exists at every moment or only at separated instants — not about whether the amplitudes are analog or digital.</div>

## 1. Time Axis: Continuous-Time versus Discrete-Time

Continuous-time and discrete-time describe **where along the horizontal time axis** a signal is defined.

- **Continuous-time:** the signal can be evaluated at every real value of \(t\). It exists at every moment.
- **Discrete-time:** the signal is defined only at separated time instants, typically indexed by integers \(n\).

### EXAMPLES

| Signal | Type | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Microphone voltage \(x(t)\) | Continuous-time | Defined for every real \(t\) |
| Quarterly GNP data \(x[n]\) | Discrete-time | Reported once per quarter only |

#### Exam Note

> **Do not decide analog/digital from the horizontal axis.** The time axis tells you continuous-time or discrete-time only.

## 2. Amplitude Axis: Analog versus Digital

Analog and digital describe the **possible vertical-axis amplitude values**, not the time axis.

- **Analog:** amplitudes may take any value in a continuous range — infinitely many possibilities.
- **Digital:** amplitudes are restricted to a **finite set** of allowed values.

### QUICK CHECK

> Ask: **how many amplitude values are allowed?** If infinitely many → analog. If a finite count → digital.

### EXAMPLE

A signal whose amplitude can only be 0 V, 1 V, 2 V, or 3 V is **digital** — even if it is drawn over a continuous time axis. The time axis does not change this verdict.

#### Remember

Analog/digital is a **vertical-axis** question. Continuous-time/discrete-time is a **horizontal-axis** question. They are independent.

$$x \in [a,\, b]$$
*This interval notation represents the **analog** idea: the amplitude \(x\) may be any real value in the continuous range from \(a\) to \(b\).

- \(a\) = lower amplitude limit; \(b\) = upper amplitude limit.
- Use this when the problem says the signal **can take any value between** two bounds.
- **Exam trigger:** phrases like "any value between", "continuous range", or "real-valued amplitude".
- **Common misuse:** thinking the time curve must be smooth. Analog refers to amplitude, not to time smoothness.*

$$x \in \{a_1,\, a_2,\, \ldots,\, a_M\}$$
*This set notation represents the **digital** idea: the amplitude \(x\) must be exactly one of \(M\) allowed values.

- \(a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_M\) are the allowed amplitude levels.
- \(M\) is the total number of levels (called **M-ary**).
- Use this when the problem says **finite levels**, **quantized levels**, or **M-ary**.
- **Common misuse:** thinking digital always means only two levels. Digital signals may have any finite number \(M\) of levels.*

$$M = 2$$
***Binary** is the special case of a digital signal with exactly **two** allowed amplitude levels.

- This is the familiar computer-style two-level signal (e.g., 0 V and 5 V, or logic LOW and HIGH).
- **Common misuse:** calling every digital signal binary. A digital signal has \(M\) levels; binary is only the case \(M = 2\). A 4-ary signal (\(M = 4\)) is digital but not binary.*

## 3. Analog-to-Digital Conversion: Quantization

A/D conversion changes an analog amplitude into one of a **finite set of digital levels**. The key operation is **quantization**: rounding or mapping each amplitude value to the nearest allowed level.

### HOW IT WORKS

If the allowed levels are 0 V, 1 V, 2 V, and 3 V, then a measured amplitude of 2.7 V is rounded to **3 V** — the nearest allowed level. The exact analog value is replaced by the closest digital level.

### KEY INSIGHT

Quantization is a **vertical-axis** operation. It changes amplitude values, not time instants.

#### Exam Note

> **Sampling affects time; quantization affects amplitude.** These are two separate steps in A/D conversion.

$$x_q = Q(x)$$
*\(Q\) is the **quantizer function** and \(x_q\) is the **quantized output amplitude**.

- \(x\) = the original analog amplitude value.
- \(x_q\) = the nearest allowed digital level after rounding.
- Use this when an analog value is mapped to a finite set of allowed levels.
- **Common misuse:** treating quantization as choosing sample times. Quantization only changes **vertical amplitude values** — it does not affect when the signal is sampled.*

### Interactive Check

Use the demo below to separate the two operations: switch time to sampling, then switch amplitude to quantization.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- **Time axis** uses \(x(t)\) (continuous-time, every real \(t\)) or \(x[n]\) (discrete-time, integer index \(n\)) — independent of amplitude.
- **Amplitude axis** uses \(x \in [a, b]\) for analog (any value in a continuous range) or \(x \in \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_M\}\) for digital (\(M\) finite levels).
- **Binary** is the special case \(M = 2\); all binary signals are digital, but not all digital signals are binary.
- **Quantization** \(x_q = Q(x)\) maps analog amplitudes to allowed digital levels — it affects the vertical axis, not the time axis.

*Next, we will classify signals by repetition: periodic versus aperiodic.*
